statement: The Constitutional Council's 2011 draft constitution includes a provision stating that compulsory military service may never be introduced into law. This strengthens the existing constitutional position: Article 75 of the current 1944 Constitution permits conscription in cases of 'national danger', though it has never been exercised in practice. Iceland has no standing military and has never had compulsory military service. The draft provision would make the prohibition absolute, removing even the theoretical possibility. This clause has gained renewed relevance in the EU membership debate, as opponents argue that evolving EU defence integration (PESCO, European Defence Fund, Strategic Compass) could eventually lead to pressure for military contributions from all member states, though the EU has no mechanism to impose conscription on member states.
source_name: Stjórnlagaráð — A Proposal for a new Constitution for the Republic of Iceland (English translation); Constitute Project — Iceland 2011D
caveats: EU membership does not require military conscription — Ireland, Austria, and Malta are EU members with varying forms of military neutrality. CSDP participation is voluntary. However, the EU's defence ambitions are evolving rapidly post-Ukraine, and future developments are uncertain. The draft constitution's prohibition is not yet in force as the draft has not been adopted.

related_entries.statement: The Constitutional Council's 2011 draft constitution includes a natural resources clause (auðlindaákvæðið) stating: 'Iceland's natural resources which are not in private ownership are the common and perpetual property of the nation. No one may acquire the natural resources or their attached rights for ownership or permanent use, and they may never be sold or mortgaged.' The clause envisions a licensing regime with leases limited to 'a modest period of time.' This provision was the subject of Question 2 in the 2012 referendum ('Do you wish that a new Constitution include a provision on national ownership of natural resources, as is in the draft?'), which received 82.9% yes — the highest approval of any of the six questions. The provision is considered economically significant as Iceland's key export sectors (fishing, energy-intensive industry, tourism) rely on natural resources.

Article 50 of the Treaty on European Union (TEU) provides that any member state may decide to withdraw from the Union in accordance with its own constitutional requirements. The withdrawal process involves a two-year negotiation period, extendable by unanimous agreement of the European Council.

citations.report_title: Treystu þjóðinni — Opið bréf til forsætisráðherra Íslands

claims.claim_text: Í tillögunum að nýrri stjórnarskrá var ákvæði um að herskyldu mægi aldrei í lög leiða.
Herskylduákvæðið í tillögunum tengist möguleikanum á því að Ísland gangi í ESB og evrópskur her yrði stofnaður.
